coredata.dc:title: An efficient pseudocapacitor electrode material with co-doping of iron (II) and sulfur in luminescent graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Kharangarh, Poonam R.
Gupta, Vinay
Singh, Amrita
Bhardwaj, Preetam
Grace, Andrews Nirmala
coredata.dc:description: An efficient electrode material (Fe (II)S-GQDs) has been prepared by co-doping of sulfur and iron atoms with Graphene quantum dots. A top down synthesis method including a facile and one-pot hydrothermal was used to develop high capacity and good stability electrode material by selecting ferrous sulphate powder (FeSO4.7H2O) and graphene oxide (GO) as a precursor material. The results obtained from TEM measurements reveal the size alters from micrometer graphene sheets of GO to nano-meter dots for GQDs and Fe (II)S-GQDs. The electrochemical studies reveal that Fe(II) S doped GQDs can exhibit a reversible redox reaction with excellent specific capacitance of 476.2 F/g as compared to that of GQDs (143F/g). It has been found that there is an improvement in electrical conductivity and electron transfer in addition to the largest charge storage capacity. More remarkably, the doped electrode material shows a small charge transfer resistance (Rct) value (30 Ω) in case of Fe(II)S-GQDs as compared to GQDs (2.5 kΩ) due to fast diffusion of ion and electron between working electrodes and electrolyte.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Hydrothermal method
Electrochemical study
TEM

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Special Section on ‘ICNAN’19-2nd International Conference on Nanoscience and Nanotechnology’

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of graphene oxide and Fe(II)-S doped GQDs
Preparation of electrodes for electrochemical measurements

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of (a) GO, and HRTEM of (b) GQDs, and (c) Fe(II)S-GQDs.
(a) XRD patterns; (b) FTIR Spectrum; (c) Raman Spectrum; and (d) TGA of GO, GQDs, & Fe(II)S-GQDs.

(a) UV–Vis of (i) GO, (ii) GQDs, and (iii) Fe(II)S-GQDs; (b) The photoluminescence spectra of GQDs, (c) Fe(II)S-GQDs using different excitation wavelengths and (d) Photoluminescence excitation spectra of Fe(II)S-GQDs.

(a) Cyclic Voltammetry of Fe(II)S-GQDs at different scan rates; (b) Galvanostatic charge-discharge(GCD) of Fe(II)S-GQDs at 1A/g current density; and (c) Nyquist Plot of Fe(II)S-GQDs, the inset of the figure shows the Nyquist Plot of GQDs.
Summary on the fabricated graphene-based supercapacitor devices.

head.ce:title: An efficient pseudocapacitor electrode material with co-doping of iron (II) and sulfur in luminescent graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics and AstroPhysics, University of Delhi, Delhi 110007, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics and AstroPhysics, University of Delhi, Delhi, 110007, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centre for Nanotechnology Research, School of Electronics Engineering, Vellore Institute of Technology University, Vellore 632 014, Tamil Nadu, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Centre for Nanotechnology Research, School of Electronics Engineering, Vellore Institute of Technology University, Vellore, 632 014, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centre for Nanotechnology Research, Vellore Institute of Technology University, Vellore 632 014, Tamil Nadu, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Centre for Nanotechnology Research, Vellore Institute of Technology University, Vellore, 632 014, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An efficient electrode material (Fe (II)S-GQDs) has been prepared by co-doping of sulfur and iron atoms with Graphene quantum dots. A top down synthesis method including a facile and one-pot hydrothermal was used to develop high capacity and good stability electrode material by selecting ferrous sulphate powder (FeSO4.7H2O) and graphene oxide (GO) as a precursor material. The results obtained from TEM measurements reveal the size alters from micrometer graphene sheets of GO to nano-meter dots for GQDs and Fe (II)S-GQDs. The electrochemical studies reveal that Fe(II) S doped GQDs can exhibit a reversible redox reaction with excellent specific capacitance of 476.2 F/g as compared to that of GQDs (143F/g). It has been found that there is an improvement in electrical conductivity and electron transfer in addition to the largest charge storage capacity. More remarkably, the doped electrode material shows a small charge transfer resistance (Rct) value (30 Ω) in case of Fe(II)S-GQDs as compared to GQDs (2.5 kΩ) due to fast diffusion of ion and electron between working electrodes and electrolyte.

ce:section.ce:para: Currently, Graphene Quantum (GQDs), a fragment or monolayer of carbon has been much more popular in many applications because of their high luminescence, less hazardous, large scale production, and easy preparation. GQDs have been widely used in bioimaging [1], photovoltaic devices [2], optoelectronic devices [3], biosensors [3], photocatalysis [3], sensors [3–5], energy storage and conversion [6–8], and drug delivery [9]. Furthermore the induction of band gap in GQDs can be artificially introduced by adding the surface functionalities or moities leading to a strong photoluminescence (PL) [10–15] with higher intensity due to their edge, doping or co-doping and quantum confinement effects.

Doping of heteroatoms in GQDs has been reported with different dopants like N, Se, S, Cl, F, B, K to yield N-/, S-/, Se-/, Cl-/, F-/, B-/, and K-/ doped GQDs [10–20]. The introduction of defect energy levels with the doping of heteroatoms alters the band gap from LUMO (π) to HOMO level (π*) [21]. Some more work related to co-doping in GQDs with S-N/N-S doped GQDs [22,23], B, N-/ doped GQDs [24], N, P-/ doped GQDs [25] and F, S, N-/doped GQDs [26] was done to make an improvement in electron density as well as for optimizing the luminescent bahaviour of GQDs.

Nowadays, the consumption of energy is rapidly increasing as a result of increase of environmental pollution with more population. To make the environment clean, more effective, and pollution free, we really need some devices to store the related energy improve the efficiency of energy system. The most demanding devices related to energy storage, Supercapacitors, have been strongly attracted by the researchers due to their high energy density, fast rate capability for charging and discharging processes and ultra-long life cycle. Based on various applications of energy storage device in many fields, super-capacitors, a new energy storage system are widely used in electrical vehicles, industrial energy management, and power back-up systems. Meanwhile, the drawbacks of these super capacitors are low surface area and low energy density so far in comparison to rechargeable batteries. To overcome this limitation, we need appropriate materials which lead to satisfactory electrochemical performance and life cycles by controlling its reduced pore size and small surface area. Due to huge area related to surface, low resistivity, and superior mechanical property, GQDs doped with different transition metal oxides are the best choice for the next generation flexible supercapacitors. As a result, performance of the psuedocapacitor electrode material can be improved which were hydrothermally prepared by GQDs doped with metal oxides for energy storage application as supercapaciotrs. However, the doped GQD materials with water soluble transition metal oxides as supercapacitor electrodes after the reduction in size to nano-scale is an effective approach to make the path short between the movement of ions or electrodes with the electrolyte. Hence, new modified strategy can offer high capacity electrode material with long stability of the material by well controlled size, shape for the hydrothermally prepared high quality GQDs.

Liu et al. [27] employed an approach for two different kind of supercapacitors (i) symmetric micro-supercapacitor (GQDs//GQDs) and (ii) asymmetric supercapacitor for electrode material of GQDs//MnO2. Liu and his group et al. [27] synthesized asymmetric supercapacitor of GQDs//MnO2 electrode material; where MnO2 nanoneedles were used as cathodic electrode and for the anodic electrode, GQDs was elected in 1 M Na2SO4 aqueous electrolyte. Liu et al. [27] reported an electro-deposition method to synthesize of symmetric micro-supercapacitor for GQDs//GQDs electrode material. As a result, the prepared asymmetric supercapacitor shows higher capacity with high stability in comparison to symmetric micro-supercapacitor with the same electrolyte. Zhang et al. [6] in 2018 reported the high specific capacitance (Csp) with high energy density (E), a small relaxation time (τ), a low internal resistance (Ri), and an excellent cycling stability for uniform size GQD (~5 nm) electrode material; which was prepared by top down strategy. Y Ouyang et al. in 2019 [23] have developed graphene quantum dots co-doping with elements of sulfur and nitrogen (S, N-GQDs) by using a top-down strategy that exhibit a good capacity for an excellent performance of specific capacitance characteristics (Csp ~ 362.60 F/ g) at a specific scan rate ~ (5 mV/s). In another study, GQD-HNT (GQDs-halloysite nanotubes) nano-composites were fabricated to enhance the electrochemical meachanism providing great performance in terms of supercapacitor applications with precursor material (3-aminopropyl)-triethoxysilane [28]. The prepared GQD-HNT composite shows specific capacitance (Csp ~ 323 F/g) at the same scan rate, while specific capacitance obtained from thin film of GQDs is around 108 F/g; which was prepared on glassy carbon as a working electrode. H. Xia [29] et al. fabricated hematite Quantum-Dot/Functionalized Graphene-Sheet composite as an anode material for asymmetric supercapacitor applications. J. Liu et al. [30] used facile solvothermal method for fabrication of Fe2O3 NDs@NG in aspect of enhancing the electrochemical performance with superior capacitance retention for long term cycling stability.
Unfortunately, most metal oxide in alkaline solutions doped with GQDs is unstable leading to the low performance in electrochemical measurements with poor life cycle.

In this paper, we report the influence of doping with Sulfur and iron in GQDs by selecting FeSO4.7H2O Powder and GO as a precursor materials by using hydrothermal treatment. In order to achieve the high performance electrode materials for super capacitor applications, iron and sulfur oxide electrode material with high rate capability has been carefully chosen due to suitable voltage window and high calculated specific capacitance. But high energy density and small surface area is a great issue which is far away from our expectations. In contrast to this problem, we reported a facile hydrothermal treatment under a top down approach which was used to prepare Fe(II)S-GQDs with graphite powder and ferrous sulphate powder for the first time as per our knowledge. At the same time, influences of doping of iron and sulfur in GQDs is useful to control the movement of ions into the electro-active sites and thus improving the electrochemical reaction for life cycle with high specific capacitance.

Natural graphite powder (Quaikema Lab), KMnO4, (98%, Alfa Aesar), 35% H2O2 (99.9%, Alfa Aesar), NaNO3 (98%, Alfa Aesar) and FeSO4.7H2O (99.3%, Sisco Research Lab), and DI water were used during all experiments. Glassy electrode, Platinum electrode and Ag/AgCl reference electrode were purchased from Indiamart (Delhi, India).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of graphene oxide and Fe(II)-S doped GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Graphite powder was prepared by using materials via. modified Hummers and Offemann [31,32]. Firstly, 2.78 mg of FeSO4 was dissolved in GO solution & deionized (DI) water (vol. ratio ~ 1:1) to make a preparation of Fe(II)S-GQDs. Then, the mixture was stirred with 30 min for the complete oxidation of graphite at room temperature (RT) and the prepared mixture was cleaned up with ultra-sonication for another 30 min. The desired product was then transferred into a Teflon lined stainless autoclave. After that, the heating temperature at 150 °C was set for the sealed autoclave within next 5 h. Furthermore, the resulting mixture was brought down at normal temperature. Finally, the observed Fe(II)S-GQDs were separated through a 0.22 μm pore size membrane filter. A similar hydrothermal method was used for synthesis of GQDs by selecting graphene oxide (GO) as a precursor material.

“High Resolution Transmission Electron Microscope” (HRTEM) images have been obtained by using FEI Technai G2 T30 electron microscope operating at 200 kV. Powdered X-Ray Diffraction (PXRD) spectra were recorded with a Rigaku up to 50 W microfocus X-ray tube using Cu or Mo radiation. “Raman spectra” were recorded using a “Renishaw Laser Raman spectrometer via microscope system” equipped with an Ar ± laser (λ = 514 nm). “Fourier Transform Infrared spectrum” (FTIR) spectrum was examined by using “PerkinElmer Spectrum RXI-Mid IR operating” (resolution ~1 cm−1) for the present functional groups. UV–Visible absorption spectra were analyzed by a “Perkin Elmer Lambda 35 spectrophotometer”. “Fluoromax-4 Spectro-fluorometer” was used to characterize the Photoluminescence (PL) spectra. “Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) of samples was measured by a Thermal analysis (Perkin Elmer) at a heating rate of 10 °C min−1 in Argon from 300 °C to 900 °C”.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of electrodes for electrochemical measurements
ce:section.ce:para: All Electrochemical measurements were performed by using an electrochemical working station (CHI760D) with three-electrode system in a 1 M K4FeCN6 aqueous electrolyte at room temperature. A glassy electrode (GE) was used as the working electrode, platinum wire as the counter electrode and silver (Ag/AgCl) electrode as reference one. In brief, firstly, the glassy electrode (GE) was cleaned up by acetone, isopropyl alcohol, and ethanol for more than 30 min, successively, before the electrochemical deposition. The slurry was prepared by mixing Fe(II)S-GQDs as an active material (wt ~ 80%), acetylene black carbon (wt ~ 15%) and polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) (wt ~ 5%) with a few drops of acetone in a petri dish. As a result, the uniform black powder was formed that will be pasted onto glassy electrode (surface area ~ 0.07 cm2). Finally, the working electrode was dried in an open air.

Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and galvanostatic charging or discharging tests were performed with different scan rates from 10 mV to 100 mV in the potential window rage from −0.3 to 0.6 V (vs. Ag/AgCl). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopic (EIS) measurements were carried out to study the ohmic resistance for the electrode system which could provide the information about the redox reaction resistance and equivalent series resistance. Also, charge transfer resistance (Rct) was employed in the frequency domain (range ~10−2–105 Hz). The specific capacitance can be calculated by using the as earlier reported equation [32] from the galvanostatic discharge graph.

Fig. 1(a–c) shows the “TEM” or “HRTEM” images of GO, GQDs, and Fe(II),S-GQDs respectively. The size of Fe(II)S–GQDs shown in Fig. 1(c) is estimated to be 5–10 nm in diameter, which seems to be much reduced than the size (~20 nm) of GQDs {Fig. 1(b)} that was synthesized by cutting GO sheets and micro-meter sheets of GO {Fig. 1(a)} has been followed by modified Hummer's methods.

This reveals that after the hydrothermal treatment, more defects related to iron and sulfur atoms produced on nano graphene sheet which is more useful to reduce the size of iron and sulfur particles. The results illustrate that formation of Fe(II)S–GQDs have been attained with adsorption of Fe, S on the lattice sites of GQDs.

Fig. 2(a) demonstrates XRD patterns of prepared GO, hydrothermally prepared GQDs and Fe(II)S-GQDs. GO powder shows a broad and strong peak near 2Ɵ = 10·9° which is attributed to (002) plane confirming the complete oxidation. The appearances of broad peak in GO is due to the existence of oxygen moities on both sides of nano graphene sheets. As a result, there is an increase in distance between carbon sheets as well as irregularities of atomic scale from sp3 bonding in carbon [33,34].

After the hydrothermal treatment, the sharp peak shifts suggests the conversion of GO to GQDs. The observed broad, peak in GQDs with 002 plane present at 2 Ɵ = 26·7° [35] is possibly due to the reduction in size and broken regular stacking. The XRD pattern of Fe(II)S–GQDs consists of strong reflections with low-intensity at 21·9°, 26.7°, 33.2°, 35.8°, 49.7°, and 54.4° which are assigned to the (111), (002) and (311) (119) (0012) (2212) planes, respectively, to a face-centered cubic structure, which is consistent with the previous reported values [10,32]. However, a weak intensity of peaks in Fe(II)S–GQDs confirms that the possibility of multilayer graphene sheets are irrelevant. The obtained results are similar as earlier reported [36,37] one. This also agrees with the crystalline structure due to the addition of some extra defects related to Fe and S along the surface of Fe(II)S-GQDs. Some of the characteristic peaks of GQDs is vanished due to face to face stacking direction was broken by introducing the Fe and S related nanoparticles on nanosheets of graphene (or GQDs) during the hydrothermal process.

FT-IR spectroscopy was used to characterize the spectra of “GO”, “GQDs” and Fe(II)S-GQDs is shown in Fig. 2(b). From the FTIR spectrum {Fig. 2(b)} the “vibrational peaks” were obtained at “CO (~1074 cm−1), COH (~1397 cm−1), aromatic carbons (~1634 cm−1), CH (~2927 cm−1), and stretching vibrational bands of COH at 3259 cm−1” in GO which is similar to our previous work [10,12,32]. Some additional peaks were observed for GQDs and Fe(II) S-GQDs. The observed vibrational bands of GQDs were seen at 522, 830, 888, 1002, 1397–1634 and 3259 cm−1, while the detected peaks in Fe(II) S-GQDs were at 466, 535, 673, 831, 1161, 1397, 1491, 1642, 2331, and 3259 cm−1. Two common peaks were observed in GQDs and Fe(II)S-GQDs at 1397 and 1642 cm−1 are related to CC and CC bonding [38,39]. The bands at 673 and 1161 cm−1 correspond to the “CS and CS bonds” show the sulfur has been completely doped into the GQDs with the strong bonding after hydrothermal treatment [40,41]. On the other hand, the absorption peak to be found at 535 cm−1 is related to the stretching vibration of the FeO bond [40,41] indicating the successful doping of iron as well. The absorption peak at 466 cm−1 is related to stretching vibrations of CS and 1022 cm−1 absorption peak was allotted to “stretching vibrations of SO”. This study confirms that the bonding of sulfur and iron atoms is consistent with other reported results [42–45]. Hence, the obtained results completely verify the formation of superior Fe(II)S-GQDs by introducing the iron and sulfur related defects on the surface of carbon matrix.

“Raman spectroscopy”, a popular technique, is used to investigate the disorders and defects. Raman spectrum, shown in Fig. 2(c), was recorded for GO, GQDs and Fe(II)-S doped GQDs. The well- known, “D-band” and “G-band” has been assigned for two prominent bands for GO, GQDs and Fe(II)-S doped GQDs at 1366 cm−1 and 1595 cm−1 respectively. However, the characteristics peak of D band is due to presence of defects created by heteroatoms doping but Raman active E2g mode is related to G bands [46]. Due to presence of sulfur and iron related defects, the additional peaks in Fe(II) and S co-doped GQDs shows the messy structure including carbon, sulfur and iron oxide bonds. The intensity ratio values of the characteristics peak for D and G bands (ID/IG) defines the randomness in structure and more defects of carbon material [47]. The intensity ratio values of GO, GQDs and Fe(II)-S doped GQDs are 0·90, 0·99 and 1.02, respectively. The calculated valve of ID/IG for Fe(II)S–GQDs is found to be more than GO and GQDs, which clearly states that the intercalation of iron and sulfur atoms into the graphene sheets provide a diordered structure. In addition, the specific vibration modes exhibit centered at 301 (B1g), 509 (B1g + A1g) and 629 cm−1 (Eg), and 956 cm−1 indicating the presence of the iron and sulfur related defects, which is similar with the results obtained from XRD. Moreover, after doping of FeSO4 in GQDs, the intensity of the Raman peaks of 509 cm−1 with greater intensity ratio values for “D band” and “G band”, in comparison to that GQDs and GO. The cutting sheets of graphene in nano-scale and doping in GQDs exhibit the presence of sulfur and iron related defects which also certify our TEM results. Hence, a large amount of sulfur and iron doping can cause a 2D peak at 2717 cm−1. Also, another defect activated peak is observed at about 2935 cm−1, in Fe(II)S-GQDs conforming the grouping mode of the “D + D′” peak (or D and D′ modes). These results again confirm the I2D/IG ratio to evaluate the doping concentration of sulfur and iron related defects in GQDs which is correlated to the non-uniform distribution of dopants.

The study of thermal stability for synthesized GO, GQDs, Fe(II)-S doped GQDs was done by thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) which is shown in Fig. 2(d) respectively. The maximum loss in weight from TGA plots was detected for GO (84%), GQDs (50%), and Fe(II)-S co-doped GQDs (40%).The calculated weight loss of GQDs is 34% and of Fe(II)-S codoped GQDs(34%) with respect to the precursor material as GO before the hydrothermal process from the weight difference. This is because of a small extent of moieties related to sulfur and iron elements. Hence, the quality of material gives remarkable changes in Fe(II) S-GQDs after heated up at high temperature, thus, this prepared material has good thermal stability in comparison to GO and GQDs.
UV–Vis absorption spectra was recorded for GO, GQDs and Fe(II)S–GQDs in aqueous media as shown in Fig. 3(a). Two peaks located at 212 nm and 251 nm were observed in GO as earlier reported [32].

"The π-π* transitions of aromatic CC bonds corresponds to the experiential peak at 212 nm and the n-π* transitions of CO bond is resulted due to peak displayed at 251 nm" [30]. The red shift phenomenon for undoped GQDs is characterized by two absorption peaks at 260 and 320 nm in comparison to GO. The existence of the “n band” corresponds to the functional groups correlated with oxygen in between the π-band and π*-band. After doping with ferrous sulphate, multiple peaks have been observed at 205 nm, and 298 nm, and 305 nm nano size nm. In other words, we can say that blue shift is observed after doping with ferrous sulphate in GO. Furthermore, the absorption peak at 298 nm and 305 nm exhibits to the n-π* transitions of the Fe(II)S-GQDs due to the trapping of excited state energy by the surface state of GQDs incorporated with iron (Fe) and sulfur related impurities resulting in strong emission. It can be observed that the absorption of the Fe(II)S-GQDs is higher as compared to GQDs which improve the absorption in visible light.

The photoluminescence (PL) spectra for GQDs and Fe(II)S-GQDs were observed under various excitation wavelengths are shown in Fig. 3(b) and (c). The PL spectrum for GQDs reveals that the PL intensity increases with an increase in excitation wavelengths (~300-360 nm) by selecting an interval of 20 nm. Also, the observed PL data of Fe(II)S-GQDs were taken with different excitation wavelengths from 290 nm to 330 nm with an interval of 10 nm. It has been found that GQDs demonstrate the maximum PL spectral emission at 434 nm, while the maximum PL emission of Fe(II)S-GQDs were observed at 396 nm with an excitation wavelength of 310 nm exhibits the excitation wavelength dependent. These results indicate that some of the particle distribution is non-uniform, but rest of the particles in Fe(II)S-GQDs are uniform.

The additional energy levels and electronic transitions have been created by defects states in GQDs after doping as so far we know. PL excitation dependence wavelength phenomenon has been observed in GQDs after doping [48].

In addition, the obtained energy levels between valence and conduction band for GQDs were observed at 434 nm with different excitations, while for Fe(II)S-GQDs these levels shifted at 360 (~2.73 eV), and 394 nm (~2.63 eV). The PL emission was generated by these additional energy levels after releasing from surface states. Furthermore, change in absorption of light and peak position of PL is responsible to modify the band structure. The higher PL peak intensity was observed in Fe(II)S-GQDs under 310 nm excitation wavelength with 394 nm emission wavelength. As a result, there is a shift in peak position of PL spectra of Fe(II)S-GQDs in comparison to un doped GQDs. Fig. 3(d) shows PLE spectrum which exhibits a sharp peak at 310 nm in sample of Fe(II)S-GQDs respectively which confirms our UV–Vis data. The dopants related to Fe and S atoms significantly enhances the intensity of fluorescence with creation of added energy states led to new electron localized levels in the energy band diagram of GQDs by introduction for additional “defect sites”.

The electrodes were prepared by a homogeneous mixture of 80 wt% of Fe(II)S-GQDs as an active material, 15 wt% acetylene black carbon and 5 wt% polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) with a few drops of acetone as a solvent. The acetylene black carbon was used to increase the conductivity of the active material whereas PTFE act as a binder. The electrochemical properties of Fe(II)S–GQDs were observed through a three electrode cell system with 0.1 M K4FeCN6 electrolyte including electrodes of glassy carbon as a working, Ag/AgCl as a reference and Platinum as a counter. The cyclic voltammograms (CV) of GQDs is already shown somewhere else in ref. [30]. A similar graph of CV curves with high current density for Fe(II)S–GQDs were recorded from −0.3 V to 0.6 V with various scan rates from 5 to 100 mV/s. In CV scan of Fe (II) S–GQDs, a cathodic peak at 0·5 V and an anodic peaks at 0·2 V were identified with different scan rates showing the faradic redox behavior {Fig. 4(a)}. The anion peak of the redox pair is responsible for the oxidation of sulfur ions, whereas the reduction process is due to cation peak ensuing the faradic reduction reactions of sulfur ion. The cathode peak shifts toward more positive potentials is owing to the existence of oxygen moities. Also, at the same time, the active materials of iron oxides are found to be more unstable when they react with aqueous electrolyte because of hydrogen evolution at the both (negative and positive) potential which leads to the weak contribution for providing high capacity. In addition, the current density increases with greater than before scan rate, which indicates a good rate capability of the Fe(II)S–GQDs compared to GQDs. This typical well-known phenomenon of the charge storage in terms of pseudo-capacitance generating from the reversible redox reactions of cations and anions from the CV curves.

The anomalous specific capacitance was evaluated by cyclic voltammetry of Fe(II)S-GQDs which was compared by observed capacitance from charge or discharge curve is shown in Fig. 4(b). The value of capacitance for Fe(II)S-GQDs electrodes can be found by selecting the integral area of CV curve. A decrease in capacitance has been observed as we moved from lower to higher scan rates in CV scan. At lower scan rates the electrolyte ions have sufficient time to penetrate the pores of the material, while at higher scan rates only it accumulates on the outer surface. As a result, the specific capacitance decreases with an increase in scan rate.

In order to achieve the information for the electrical resistance from electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements, Nyquist plots were drawn as shown in Fig. 4(c). “The diameter of the semicircle at high frequency is related to the charge transfer between the electrode material and the electrolyte, and the tail slope at low frequency is related to the ionic diffusion rate in the electrolyte” as clearly mentioned in refs [45, 46]. The observation of a single semicircular arc in Nyquist plots (Z″ against Z′) confirms the presence of bulk effects only. The more straight the route in the impedance plot, the supercapacitor acts as an ideal capacitor more closely. The tail slope was 30 Ω for Fe(II)S–GQDs and 2·5 k Ω for GQDs [32], which indicate that the ion diffusion rate of Fe(II)S–GQDs is higher than that of GQDs. Fe(II) S–GQDs, This is due to fast movement of efficient ion and electrons; resulting in high charge–discharge capacities with a particular current density (~1 mA/g). Thus, the reduced current density stimulates the ions to enter inside the active sites of the electrode and relatively high specific capacitance of Fe(II)S–GQDs was obtained as compared to GQDs.
A comparison Table I for all different electrode materials doped with GQDs were mentioned below by giving the brief description of specific capacitance with cycles at different current density.

These results show superior electrode material which might be a great involvement with excellent capacitive behavior in applied applications for next generation. The galvanostatic charge–discharge curve of GQDs and Fe(II)S–GQDs were performed as shown in Fig. 4(b). The specific capacitance was calculated from the discharge curve in accordance with the following equation [32] The specific capacitance (C) of Fe(II)S–GQDs is found to be 476.2 F/g, which is three times greater in comparison to GQDs (C = 143 F/g) [54]. This is attributed to the fact that the nano-scale size and edge effects of Fe(II) S–GQDs overcome the restacking of nano size graphene sheets (or so called GQDs) led to the high electrochemical activity with higher conductivity between the electrolyte ions and active electrode material. The electron transportation from structure to the current collector promotes the most efficient exposure of active sites to the electrolyte which can be verified by the small series resistance shown in Fig. 4(c).

In summary, a chemical route was performed to develop Fe(II)-S GQDs electrode material via hydrothermal method by using low-cost materials like graphite powder and FeSO4.7H2O. These doped GQDs shows crystallinity and water dispersability. Besides, Fe(II)-S GQDs exhibit excitation dependent PL behavior under the 310 nm excitation UV–Vis absorption light. Interestingly, the optical measurements like FTIR, UV–Vis, Raman and PL spectroscopic, were strongly demonstrates the active dopants related to sulfur and iron elements to form Fe(II)S–GQDs electrode. Specific capacitance (Csp) of Fe(II)S–GQDs was attained up to 476.2 F/g which is greater than the reported doped and co-doped GQDs. It was demonstrated, for the first time, as per our knowledge, that the water soluble Fe(II)S–GQD electrodes exhibits two pair of different peaks results in a reversible redox behavior due to the presence of sulfur atoms. Meanwhile, Fe(II)S–GQD shows the remarkable performance of pseudocapacitance with doping of iron and sulfur in GQDs where the dopants act as trap energy level to boost the capacity of charge storage for energy related applications. We believe that the water-soluble Fe(II)S–GQD electrodes provides their new direction for charge storage mechanism with superior pseudocapacitors in the near future.

Poonam R. Kharangarh: Conceptualization, Methodology, Visualization, Writing - original draft, Investigation. Vinay Gupta: Supervision. Amrita Singh: Writing - review & editing. Preetam Bhardwaj: Visualization. Andrews Nirmala Grace: Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The financial support for this work was provided by “Department of Science & Technology”, New Delhi under “Women Scientist Scheme A” (DST WOS-A) (Ref. No.: SR/WOS-A/PM-107/2017). The authors thank to EMDL Lab members in University of Delhi for facilitating us in many characterization.
